title: practical english usage - Part 292
content:
‘ Where's Jack? ‘ Somewhere around/about!

Stop standing around/about and do some work.

The children were running around/about everywhere.

'in different parts of, ‘ somewhere in’  and similar ideas.

positions that are not very clear or definite: ‘ here and there’ , ‘ in lots of places’ , We   use around or about (AmE  usually around) to refer to movements or 3 indefinite       movement        and   position Can I look (a)round?  Could you pass the cups (a)round, please?

We walked (a)round the old part of the town.

(or most) parts of a place, or giving things to everybody in a group.

We   also use round or around (AmE  usually around) to talk about going to all 2 touring,     distribution:        round ‘ Where do you live? ‘Just (a)round the corner!

Td like to travel (a)round the world.

She walked (a)round the car and looked at the wheels.

in a circle or a curve.

We   use both round and around (AmE  usually around) for movement or position 1  circular      movement,        etc:  (a)round 376 (a)round       and   about      _______________ vocabulary7 •  378   as if and as though; like He talks as if he were rich.

This is common in American English.

In a formal style, were can be used instead of was in an ‘ unreal’  comparison.

Why is he looking at me as though he knew me? I've never seen him  before.

- You look as though you know each other.

He talks as if he was rich. (But he is definitely not.) - She looks as if she is rich. (Perhaps she is.) unreality. Compare: with a present meaning after as if/though. This emphasises the meaning of When  we  talk about things which  we  know are not true, we  can use a past tense 2 tenses She was acting as if/though she was in charge.

I feel as if/though I'm dying.

They  can also be used to talk about things which  we  know are not true.

It sounds as if/though Jack's going to change his job.

It looks as if/though it's going to rain.

refer to something that we  think may be true.

As if and as though are both used to say what a situation seems like. They  can 1  meaning 378 as   if  and   as   though;       like For far + object + infinitive clause (e.g.  What I want is for everyone to have lunch), ►     ɪ  13.

I decided to stop and have lunch - for I was feeling hungry.

most common in a formal written style, and is becoming rare.

the beginning of sentences, and cannot stand alone. For, used in this sense, is afterthought. A ʃor-elause could almost be in brackets. For-clauses never come at For introduces new information, but suggests that the reason is given as an 3 for For more information about because, ►    393.

(= ... I know because I found ...) You didn't tell me the truth, because I found the money in your room.

A  because-dause can be used to say how  one knows something.

‘ Why are you laughing? ‘ Because you look so funny.' since I'm fed up!) not Why am I leaving? I'm leaving because I'm fed up! (     . .. I'm leaving as/ like this.

usually comes at the end. It can also stand alone. Since and as cannot be used When  the reason is the most important part of the sentence, the because-clause Because I was ill for six months, I lost my job.

information which  is not known to the listener/reader.

Because puts more emphasis on the reason, and most often introduces new 2  because It's raining again, so we'll have to stay at home.

are often expressed with so.

As- and sznce-clauses are relatively formal; in an informal style, the same ideas Word  Problems from A  to Z     Section 31 vocabulary •  379   as long as ► as   well,    also     and   too     ɜθθ not The train's late, as usual, (     .. .-as-uswtily;) Note that in this expression we  use the adjective usual, not the adverb usually.

I'm not a teacher as such, but I've taught English to some of my friends.

business. But I managed quite a lot of sightseeing.' ‘ So you went on a tour to Japan? ’ Well, not a tour as such -1 went on or expression suggests.

Not. .. as such is used to say that something is not exactly what a word 380 as   such For a similar use of as much/many as, ►    203.6.
